nativeRange.habitat: Creeks, swamps, streams, and pools in humid forested highland savannah and areas of rainforest; some habitats may periodically dry out but often retain water year-round

diet.notes: The Gardneri Killifish is a micropredator with a relatively large mouth for its body size. It has been documented eating a range of small live and frozen foods including daphnia, baby brine shrimp, blackworms, bloodworms, and micro-worms. Frozen foods are generally accepted readily; micropellets can be offered occasionally, though meaty foods are generally preferred. Males have been known to attempt eating very small fish, so tankmates should be of comparable size or larger.

avoidWith: other male Fundulopanchax gardneri (severe aggression between males)
other killifish species males in small tanks
nano fish smaller than ~2 cm (predation risk)

summary: The Gardneri Killifish is a vividly colored West African species prized for its iridescent blue-green flanks and red spotting. A non-annual killifish that lives several years in the aquarium, it is widely considered one of the most accessible killifish for hobbyists new to the group. Males are aggressive toward each other and are best kept one per tank.
careNotes:
Non-annual species: unlike Nothobranchius killifish — which complete their life cycle in a single wet season and require eggs to be stored dry through a simulated drought — Fundulopanchax gardneri does not require this dry-season rest period and tends to live 2–5 years in well-maintained aquariums. Although some natural habitats may periodically dry out, the species generally has access to water year-round, making it considerably more straightforward to keep.

Male aggression: males tend to be highly aggressive toward other males of the same species and will often fight to the point of injury or death in confined spaces. Keeping a single male with two or three females is the generally advised approach. If housing multiple males is desired, a larger aquarium of 100 litres or more with dense visual breaks — tall plants, driftwood, and spawning mops — may allow a colony to coexist, but close monitoring is recommended.

Female ratio: stocking two to three females per male is generally recommended to distribute male attention and reduce stress on individual females.

Escape risk: this species is considered a confirmed jumper and has been known to exit aquariums through very small gaps. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory, not optional.

Geographic and ornamental variants: several nominal subspecies are documented — F. g. lacustris (the Lake Ejagham population from Cameroon), F. g. mamfensis (from the Mamfe region of Cameroon), and F. g. nigerianus (Nigerian populations, including the deeper-blue Nigerian strain popular in the hobby). An ornamental Mama Gold strain with gold or yellow body coloration and albino strains also exist as captive-bred line variants. The Nigerian form is often described as having more intense blue coloration, while the Cameroon form — particularly mamfensis — tends toward gold or olive tones. These variants share the same husbandry requirements; no special care differences have been documented.

Hardness tolerance: unlike many West African killifish that require very soft, acidic water, Fundulopanchax gardneri has been documented tolerating moderate hardness, which makes it more adaptable to typical tap water conditions in many regions.

Cooler temperature tolerance: this species is commonly kept at lower temperatures than most tropical aquarium fish. Many keepers report good health at 20–23°C, and spawning can be triggered by a slight temperature drop. Temperatures of 25–26°C are near the upper practical limit; sustained higher temperatures are generally not recommended.

Name etymology: the word 'killi' derives from the Dutch word for 'creek' or 'channel' — not 'killer'. The species is named in honor of W. A. Gardner, under whose direction early specimens were collected. The common trade name Steel-Blue Killifish refers to the male's iridescent blue-green metallic sheen.

Egg transport: dry-incubated eggs can remain viable for 30–60 days in moist peat or spawning mop material and are commonly traded and shipped between hobbyists by mail, which has made this species a particularly accessible entry point into the killifish hobby.

Females tend to be somewhat smaller and less colorful than males, with adult size ranging from approximately 5.0 to 6.5 cm.
breedingNotes: The Gardneri Killifish is commonly considered one of the more accessible killifish to breed and is often recommended as a starting point for hobbyists new to the group. Females scatter eggs among spawning mops, fine-leaved plants, or directly on the substrate — a semi-annual strategy, meaning eggs do not require a mandatory dry period to develop. Two incubation methods have been documented: permanent submersion in water, in which eggs typically hatch in approximately 14–21 days depending on temperature, or dry incubation in moist peat or coir for approximately 3–4 weeks before wetting to trigger hatching. Eggs are reported to remain viable for 30–60 days during dry incubation, which is what allows them to be shipped between hobbyists by mail. Fry are relatively large at hatching compared to many small aquarium species and have been reported to accept micro-worms, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and commercial micro-foods from their first feeding. Separating adults from the spawning substrate or raising fry in a dedicated container is generally recommended, as adults will consume both eggs and fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for a dedicated gardneri page. SeriouslyFish is the first secondary for fish-freshwater per source-urls.json; confirmed scientific name, taxonomy, native range (Nigeria + Cameroon, Cross River/Benue systems), water parameters (20-26°C, pH 6.0-7.5, 18-179 ppm hardness), adult size (50-55mm SL), diet, subspecies, egg incubation timing, and conspecific aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), native range (Cross River / Beno, Nigeria), adult size (6.5 cm TL), water parameters (22-25°C, pH 6.0-7.2, hardness 5-8 dH). Confirmed minimum tank length 60 cm. Size and temperature values averaged with SeriouslyFish to produce composite ranges.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Partial species coverage within a top-5 killifish article (no dedicated gardneri page found). Confirmed adult size (~6 cm / 2.5 in), temperature range broadly 19-26°C, escape risk (tight lid required), breeding ease, and male feistiness / compatibility notes. Used as third source to triangulate size and temperature.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are scattered among spawning mops or fine-leaved plants and remain viable for 30–60 days under dry incubation. See breedingNotes for full details.
